title: Module Lattice Security (Part III): Structured CVP Distance on the Log-Unit Lattice
abstract: We prove that the $L^2$ CVP distance from a random short ring element to the log-unit lattice of $\Q(ζ_{2^k})$ converges to $\fracπ{2\sqrt{6}}\sqrt{n}$ as $n=2^{k-1}\to\infty$. We then show that this target lies inside the Voronoi cell of the origin for $k\ge 4$. For the $L^\infty$ norm, the maximum over $n$ sub-Gaussian coordinates yields $O(\sqrt{\log n})$ which translates into a sub-polynomial approximation factor for the Short Generator Problem. We show a Coarse Lattice Theorem that Babai's algorithm returns zero for all structured targets, yet exactly recovers unit perturbations of arbitrary size. For module determinant ideals, we further prove the Trigamma Theorem that proves an intrinsic imbalance $σ_{g_0}=O(1)$ independent of the modulus $q$. Finally, combined with Parts I and II, we reduce the CDPR factor for ML-KEM from $\exp(\tO(\sqrt{n}))$ to a sub-polynomial value.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous mathematical analysis of the security of module lattices used in post-quantum cryptography. It demonstrates that the structured nature of these lattices allows for much tighter error bounds than previously assumed, significantly reducing the estimated approximation factor for certain attacks on ML-KEM.
main_problem: Investigating the security of module lattices in post-quantum cryptography, specifically determining the CVP distance on the log-unit lattice and its impact on the ML-KEM (Kyber) scheme.
main_result: Proves that the L2 CVP distance converges to a specific constant times sqrt(n) and reduces the Short Generator Problem approximation factor from exponential to sub-polynomial.
method: Uses probabilistic methods including the Central Limit Theorem, sub-Gaussian concentration inequalities, and analytic tools like the Trigamma function and QR decomposition.
model_or_system: The mathematical framework involves cyclotomic fields Q(zeta_{2^k}), the log-unit lattice, and module lattices of rank d over the ring of integers.
key_observables: L2 and L-infinity CVP distances, approximation factor gamma, and the variance of the log-determinant sigma_{g0}.
important_parameters: Dimension n = 2^{k-1}, module rank d, and the coefficient variance sigma_c^2.
main_assumptions: Assumes ring elements have i.i.d. bounded coefficients and utilizes the Gaussian limit for large dimensions.
figures_summary: Table 4 summarizes the cumulative progress of the CDPR attack analysis across three parts of the paper, comparing prior states to the current work.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from analyzing L2 and L-infinity distances on the log-unit lattice to evaluating Babai's algorithm performance, then extends the analysis to module determinant ideals via the Trigamma Theorem, and concludes with security implications for ML-KEM.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
